title: PRESENTATION III FUNCTIONALITY OF BEAM CATEGORIES

pages.title: PRESENTATION III FUNCTIONALITY OF BEAM CATEGORIES

blocks.html:
PRESENTATION III
FUNCTIONALITY OF BEAM CATEGORIESby J.L. Verdier

In I 5, we studied the behavior of the pre-bundle categories in relation to to functors between argument categories. In this presentation, we extend this study to case of sites and categories of beams (No. 1 and 2). After introducing the topology induced (n° 3), we approach in n° 4 the comparison lemma which will play an important role in Exp. IV. In number 5, we study, for the patient reader, certain diagrams commutative functions linked to localized categories (of the type \(C/X\)). In Theorem 5.1 we do assumptions of smallness on the categories considered. These hypotheses are not, in general, satisfied by the sites encountered in practice (\(\mathcal{U}\)-sites). This requires some contortions (4.2, 4.3, 4.4).
1. Continuous functors

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 1.1

Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites and \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor between the underlying categories. We say that \(u\) is continuous if for any sheaf of sets \(F\) on \(C'\), the pre-beam \(X \mapsto F \circ u(X)\) on \(C\) is a beam.
This notion of continuity depends a priori on the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) for which the two sites are \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites. Proposition 1.5 shows that it does not depend on it.

Let us denote by \(i : \widetilde{C} \to \widehat{C}\) (resp. \(i' : \widetilde{C}' \to \widehat{C}'\)) the functor canonical inclusion of the bundles in the pre-sheaves. According to definition 1.1, the functor \(u\) is continuous if and only if there exists a functor \(u_s : \widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C}\) such that we have the equality \(i u_s = u^* i'\) (with \(u^* F = F \circ u\) (I 5.0)).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.2

Let \(C\) be a small site, \(C'\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(u : C \to C'\) a functor between the underlying categories. The following properties are equivalent:

  1. i)

    The functor \(u\) is continuous.

  2. ii)

    For any object \(X\) of \(C\) and any sieve \(R \hookrightarrow X\) covering \(X\), the morphism \(u_! R \to u(X)\) is bicovering in \(\widehat{C}'\) (II 5.2 and I 5.1).(∗)

  3. iii)

    For any bi-covering family \(H_i \to K\), \(i \in I\), of \(\widehat{C}\), the family of \(u_! H_i \to u_! K\), \(i \in I\), is bicovering in \(\widehat{C}'\).

  4. iv)

    There exists a functor \(u^s : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}'\), commuting at inductive limits and “extending \(u\)”, i.e. such as the canonical functor diagram \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{\varepsilon_C} & C' \ar[d]^-{\varepsilon_{C'}} \\ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{u^s} & \widetilde{C}' } \] is commutative up to isomorphism.

Moreover, when \(C\) is no longer necessarily a small site, but a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, we have always i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) iv) and the functor \(u^s\) of iv) is necessarily an adjoint to the left of functor \(u_s : \widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C}\), and is subsequently determined up to a unique isomorphism.
Proof: The proof of i) \(\Longleftrightarrow\) iv) when \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site will be done in 4.2. Assume \(C\) small. It is clear that iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii).

i) \(\Longrightarrow\) iii): Let \(H_i \to K\), \(i \in I\), a bicovering family of \(\widehat{C}\). For any beam \(F\) on \(C'\), the pre-beam \(u^* F\) (I 5.0) is a beam on \(C\). Therefore (II 5.3) the canonical application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(K, u^* F) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(H_i, u^* F) , \] is bijective. By addition (I 5.1), we deduce that the application canonical \[ \mathrm{Hom}(u_! K, F) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(u_! H_i, F) \] is bijective. Consequently (II 5.3) the family \(u_! H_i \to u_! K\), \(i \in I\), is double covering.

ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i): Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\), \(R \hookrightarrow X\) a covering sieve, \(F\) a beam to \(C'\). According to (II 5.3), the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(u_! X, F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(u_! R, F) \] is bijective. By addition (I 5.1), we deduce that the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X, u^* F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(R, u^* F) \] is bijective. Therefore \(u^* F\) is a sheaf.

i) \(\Longrightarrow\) iv): Let's use the usual notations \(a\) and \(i\) (resp. \(a'\) and \(i'\)) for the functors “associated bundle” and “inclusion in presheaves”. For any beam \(G\) on \(C\), let us set \(u^s G = a' u_! i G\). For any beam \(F\) on \(C'\), we have the sequence of natural isomorphisms: \[ \mathrm{Hom}(G, u_s F) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(iG, i u_s F) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(iG, u^* i'F) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(u_! iG, i'F) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(a' u_! iG, F) . \] (The first isomorphism comes from \(i\) being fully faithful; the second isomorphism comes from the definition of \(u_s\); the third and fourth isomorphism are obtained by addition). As a result \(u^s\) is deputy on the left to \(u_s\). In particular \(u^s\) switches to inductive limits tives. For any pre-beam \(K\) on \(C\) and any beam \(F\) on \(C'\), we have the sequence of natural isomorphisms: \[ \mathrm{Hom}(u^s a K, F) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(a K, u_s F) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(K, i u_s F) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(K, u^* i'F) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(u_! K, i'F) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}(a' u_! K, F) \] (the third isomorphism comes from the definition of \(u_s\); the others are obtained- nent by addition); hence a functorial isomorphism \(u^s a K \simeq a' u_! K\). In particular, using this isomorphism when \(K\) is representable, we obtain, taking into account I 5.4 3), a commutative diagram with isomor- phism near: \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{\varepsilon_C} & C' \ar[d]^-{\varepsilon_{C'}} \\ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{u^s} & \widetilde{C}' } \]

iv) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii): Let \(h : C \to \widehat{C}\) (resp. \(h' : C' \to \widehat{C}'\)) be the functor which associates the pre-beam that it represents with an object. Let's consider the diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{h} & C' \ar[d]^-{h'} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[r]^-{u_!} \ar[d]_-{a} & \widehat{C}' \ar[d]^-{a'} \\ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{u^s} & \widetilde{C}' . } \]

The top square is commutative (I 5.4 3)) and we have \(a h = \varepsilon_C\), \(a' h' = \varepsilon_{C'}\). For any object \(K\) of \(\widehat{C}\), we have a canonical isomorphism (I 3.4): \[ \varinjlim_{(h(X) \to K)\, \in\, \mathrm{ob}(C/K)} h(X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } K . \] As the functors \(u_!\), \(u^s\), \(a\) and \(a'\) commute at inductive limits, we deduce isomorphisms: \[ \varinjlim_{(h(X) \to K)\, \in\, \mathrm{ob}(C/K)} u^s a h(X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } u^s a K , \] \[ \varinjlim_{(h(X) \to K)\, \in\, \mathrm{ob}(C/K)} a' u_! h(X) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } a' u_! K . \] We have \(u_! h = h' u\) and, by hypothesis, we have an isomorphism \(a' h' u \simeq u^s a h\), hence an isomorphism: \[ u^s a K \simeq a' u_! K , \] which we immediately verify that it is functorial in \(K\). The diagram \[ \xymatrix{ \widehat{C} \ar[r]^-{u_!} \ar[d]_-{a} & \widehat{C}' \ar[d]^-{a'} \\ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{u^s} & \widetilde{C}' , } \] is therefore commutative up to isomorphism. As \(a i\) is isomorphic to the functor identical, we have an isomorphism \(u^s \simeq a' u_! i\), hence the uniqueness of \(u^s\). Either \(v : H \to K\) a bicovering morphism of \(\widehat{C}\). Then \(a(v)\) is an isomorphism (II 5.3) and consequently \(a' u_!(v)\) (isomorphic to \(u^s a(v)\)) is an isomorphism. So \(u_!(v)\) is transformed by \(a'\) into an isomorphism, and consequently \(u_!(v)\) is two-covering (II 5.3), hence ii).
The additional assertion, in the case \(C\) small, was proven in demonstration course, cqfd.

The functor \(u^s : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}'\) of 1.2 iv) can be interpreted often as a “reciprocal image” functor by a “topos morphism” \(\widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C}\), cf. IV 4.9. Its properties are summarized in the proposition next:

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.3

Let \(u : C \to C'\) be a continuous functor between a small site \(C\) and a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site \(C'\).

  1. 1)

    The functor \(u^s\) is left adjoint to the functor \(u_s\).

  2. 2)

    We have a canonical isomorphism \(u^s \simeq a' u_! i\).

  3. 3)

    We have a canonical isomorphism \(u^s a \simeq a' u_!\).

  4. 4)

    The functor \(u^s\) commutes at inductive limits.

  5. 5)

    When the functor \(u_!\) is left-exact (cf. I 5.4 4)), the functor \(u^s\) is too. More generally the functor \(u^s\) commutes to the types of finite projective limits to which the functor \(u_!\) commutes.

Proof: Assertions 1), 2), 3), 4) have been proven in the course of demonstration of 1.2. Assertion 5) results from isomorphism 2), takes into account given that \(i\) commutes at projective limits and that \(a'\) commutes with finite projective limits (II 4.1).

blocks.title: Noticed
blocks.html:
Note 1.4

By combining I 5.4 3) and 1.3 3) we obtain a diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{h} & C' \ar[d]^-{h'} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[r]^-{u_!} \ar[d]_-{a} & \widehat{C}' \ar[d]^-{a'} \\ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{u^s} & \widetilde{C}' , } \] where the top square is commutative and the bottom square is commutative to near isomorphism. But the functors \(a\), \(a'\), \(u_!\), \(u^s\) are only defined at near isomorphism. The reader will be able to verify that we can choose the functors \(a\), \(a'\) and \(u^s\) so that:

  1. 1)

    The compound functors \(a h\) and \(a' h'\) are injective on the sets of objects;

  2. 2)

    the diagram \[ \xymatrix{ C \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{a h} & C' \ar[d]^-{a' h'} \\ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{u^s} & \widetilde{C}' } \] is commutative.

More precisely, we can choose the functors \(a\) and \(a'\) so to fulfill condition 1). The functors \(a\) and \(a'\) being chosen, we can choose the functor \(u^s\) so as to satisfy condition 2).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.5

Let \(\mathcal{U} \subset \mathcal{V}\) be two universes, \(C\) and \(C'\) two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites, \(u : C \to C'\) a functor between the underlying categories. Then \(u\) is continuous relative to tively to \(\mathcal{U}\) if and only if it is continuous relative to \(\mathcal{V}\). Moreover, when \(u\) is continuous, let us denote by \(u^s_{\mathcal{U}}\) (resp. \(u^s_{\mathcal{V}}\)) the functor between categories of \(\mathcal{U}\)-bundles (resp. \(\mathcal{V}\)-bundles) introduced in 1.2 iv). So the diagram \[ (1.5.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \ar[r]^-{u^s_{\mathcal{U}}} \ar[d] & \widetilde{C}'_{\mathcal{U}} \ar[d] \\ \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}} \ar[r]_-{u^s_{\mathcal{V}}} & \widetilde{C}'_{\mathcal{V}} , } \] where the vertical functors are the canonical inclusion functors, is commutative up to canonical isomorphism.

Proof. We will only prove in the case where \(C\) is \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. The general case will be demonstrated in 4.3. It is clear that if the functor \(u^*\) transforms all \(\mathcal{V}\)-beam on \(C'\) into \(\mathcal{V}\)-beam on \(C\), it transforms everything \(\mathcal{U}\)-beam on \(C'\) to \(\mathcal{U}\)-beam on \(C\). Suppose that \(u^*\) transforms everything \(\mathcal{U}\)-beam on \(C'\) to \(\mathcal{U}\)-beam on \(C\). Let us denote \(\widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \hookrightarrow \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) (resp. \(\widehat{C}'_{\mathcal{U}} \hookrightarrow \widehat{C}'_{\mathcal{V}}\)) the categories of \(\mathcal{U}\)-prebundles and \(\mathcal{V}\)-prebundles, \(u_{\mathcal{U}!}\) and \(u_{\mathcal{V}!}\) the “reciprocal image” functors for \(\mathcal{U}\)-presheaves and \(\mathcal{V}\)-presheaves respectively. We have a commutative diagram (cf. the explicit construction from \(u_!\) in I 5.1): \[ (*) \qquad \xymatrix{ \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \ar[r]^-{u_{\mathcal{U}!}} \ar[d] & \widehat{C}'_{\mathcal{U}} \ar[d] \\ \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}} \ar[r]_-{u_{\mathcal{V}!}} & \widehat{C}'_{\mathcal{V}} . } \] The inclusion functors of \(\mathcal{U}\)-pre-sheaves in \(\mathcal{V}\)-pre-sheaves are fully faithful and commute at projective limits. It results then from (II 5.1 i)) that a bicovering morphism of \(\mathcal{U}\)-presheaves is a bicovering morphism of \(\mathcal{V}\)-presheaves. Let then \(R \hookrightarrow X\) be a sieve covering an object \(X\) of \(C\). According to 1.2 ii), the morphism \(u_{\mathcal{U}!} R \to u_{\mathcal{U}!} X\) is double covering. Using the commutativity of (*) and the above, we deduce that the morphism \(u_{\mathcal{V}!} R \to u_{\mathcal{V}!} X\) is bicovering. As a result (1.2), the functor \(u^*\) transforms the \(\mathcal{V}\)-sheaves on \(C'\) into \(\mathcal{V}\)-sheaves on \(C\). The commutativity of 1.5.1 then results from the commutativity of (*), of II 3.6 and 1.3 2).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.6

Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites, \(u : C \to C'\) a functor between the underlying categories. Let's consider the conditions:

  1. i)

    \(u\) is continuous (see 1.1 and 1.2).

  2. ii)

    For any covering family \((X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), of \(C\), the family \((u X_i \to u X)\), \(i \in I\), of \(C'\), is covering.

We have the implication i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii).

Suppose that the topology of \(C\) can be defined by a pretopology \(T\) (II 1.3) and that the functor \(u\) commutes to the fibered products.(∗) Then the conditions i) and ii) are equivalent and are equivalent to the con- next wording:

  1. iii)

    The functor \(u\) transforms the covering families of \(T\) into families coverings of \(C'\).

In particular, suppose that in \(C\) the fiber products are representable and that the functor \(u\) commutes to fiber products. So conditions i) and ii) are equivalent.

Proof. Let us show that i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii). Let \(X_i \to X\), \(i \in I\), be a covering family from \(C\). For any beam \(F\) over \(C'\), \(u^* F\) is a beam. As a result, the app- application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(X, u^* F) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(X_i, u^* F) \] is injective (II 5.1). Hence an injective application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(u_! X, F) \longrightarrow \prod_i \mathrm{Hom}(u_! X_i, F) . \] So the family \(u_! X_i \to u_! X\), \(i \in I\), is covering in \(C'\) (II 5.1).

Every covering family of the pretopology \(T\) is a family covering of \(C\), hence ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) iii). Let us show that iii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i). Let \(X\) be a object of \(C\) and \(X_i \to X\), \(i \in I\), a covering family of \(\mathrm{Cov}(X)\) (II 1.3). The morphisms \(X_i \to X\) are quatable and the functor \(u\) commutes to fiber products. As a result, the fiber products \(u(X_i) \times_{u(X)} u(X_j)\) are representable and canonically isomorphic to \(u(X_i \times_X X_j)\). Let \(F\) be a beam to \(C'\). Then the set diagram: \[ F(u(X)) \longrightarrow \prod_i F(u(X_i)) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j} F(u(X_i \times_X X_j)) \] is correct (II 2.1, I 3.5 and I 2.12). Therefore the diagram of sets \[ u^* F(X) \longrightarrow \prod_i u^* F(X_i) \rightrightarrows \prod_{i,j} u^* F(X_i \times_X X_j) \] is correct. Therefore \(u^* F\) is a sheaf (II 2.4), hence i). The last asser- tion of 1.6 results from the above and from the fact that, when the products fibers are representable in \(C\), all covering families of \(C\)(1) define on \(C\) a pretopology whose associated topology is the topology of \(C\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 1.7

Let \(C\) be a small site, \(C'\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(u : C \to C'\) a continuous functor. Let \(\gamma\) be an algebraic structure defined by limits finite projectives, such as in the category of \(\gamma\)-objects of \(\mathcal{U}\text{-Ens}\) the \(\mathcal{U}\)-inductive limits are representable.(2) We use the notations of proposition II 6.4. The functor \(u_s\) commutes at the limits projectives and therefore defines a functor \(u_s^\gamma : \widetilde{C}'_\gamma \to \widetilde{C}_\gamma\). The functor \(u_s^\gamma\) admits a left adjoint \(u^s_\gamma\) which has the properties following:

  1. 1)

    We have a canonical isomorphism \(u^s_\gamma \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } a'_\gamma u_{\gamma !} i_\gamma\), and \(u^s_\gamma\) commutes to the finite projective limits to which \(u_{\gamma !}\) commutes.

  2. 2)

    We have a canonical isomorphism \(u^s_\gamma a_\gamma \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } a'_\gamma u_{\gamma !}\).

  3. 3)

    The functor \(u^s_\gamma\) commutes at inductive limits.

  4. 4)

    If \(u^s\) is exact on the left, the diagram (notation of II 6.3.0) \[ \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C}_\gamma \ar[r]^-{u^s_\gamma} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{esj}^\sim} & \widetilde{C}'_\gamma \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{esj}'^\sim} \\ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{u^s} & \widetilde{C}' } \] is commutative up to isomorphism and the functor \(u^s_\gamma\) is exact.

  5. 5)

    Suppose that the functor \(\mathrm{esj}^\sim\) (resp. \(\mathrm{esj}'^\sim\)) has an adjoint to left \(\mathrm{Lib}^\sim\) (resp. \(\mathrm{Lib}'^\sim\)) (II 6.5). We have a canonical isomorphism: \[ u^s_\gamma \mathrm{Lib}^\sim \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Lib}'^\sim u^s . \]

Also, when \(C\) is not necessarily a small site but a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, the functor \(u_s^\gamma\) admits a left adjoint \(u^s_\gamma\). Finally if \(\mathcal{V}\) is a universe containing \(\mathcal{U}\) and if \(u^s_{\gamma, \mathcal{U}}\) (resp. \(u^s_{\gamma, \mathcal{V}}\)) designates the adjoint of left of \(u_s\) relative to the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) (resp. \(\mathcal{V}\)), the following diagram, analogous to diagram 1.5.1: \[ (1.7.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C}_{\gamma, \mathcal{U}} \ar[r]^-{u^s_{\gamma, \mathcal{U}}} \ar[d] & \widetilde{C}'_{\gamma, \mathcal{U}} \ar[d] \\ \widetilde{C}_{\gamma, \mathcal{V}} \ar[r]_-{u^s_{\gamma, \mathcal{V}}} & \widetilde{C}'_{\gamma, \mathcal{V}} } \] is commutative up to canonical isomorphism.
Proof. The demonstration, in the case where \(C\) is a small site, is left to the reader. It will be completed in 4.3 in the case where \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site.

blocks.title: Rating
blocks.html:
Rating 1.8

We will now use the notation \(u_s\) to designate the functor \(u_s^\gamma\). This notation is not likely to cause confusion, because the functor \(u_s\) (defined on the set sheaves) commutes to finite projective limits.

Similarly, when \(u^s\) is left-exact, we will use the notation \(u^s\) to denote the functor \(u^s_\gamma\). This abuse of notation is just tified by 1.7 4).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 1.9.1

Let \(X\) be a small topological space. Let us denote by \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) the open category of \(X\) (the objects of \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) are the subsets open of \(X\), the morphisms of \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) are the inclusions) provided with the canonical topology. A family \((U_i \subset U)\), \(i \in I\), is covering if and only- ment if the open \(U_i\) covers \(U\) (II 2.6). The assembly harnesses bles on \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)\) are therefore the sheaves of sets in the sense of [TF]. Let \(f : X \to Y\) be a continuous application. We deduce a functor \(f^{-1} : \mathrm{Ouv}(Y) \to \mathrm{Ouv}(X)\): for all open \(U\) of \(Y\), \(f^{-1}(U)\) is the image reciprocal of \(U\) by the application \(f\). The functor \(f^{-1}\) is a functor continuous (1.6). The functor \(f^{-1}_s : \mathrm{Ouv}(X)^{\sim} \to \mathrm{Ouv}(Y)^{\sim}\) is the functor direct image for the beams, in the sense of [TF]. The functor \((f^{-1})^s : \mathrm{Ouv}(Y)^{\sim} \to \mathrm{Ouv}(X)^{\sim}\) is the reciprocal image functor for beams within the meaning of [TF]. The functor \((f^{-1})^s\) commutes at the limits finite projectives, thanks to 1.3 5).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 1.9.2

Let \(X\) be a topological space and \(Y \hookrightarrow X\) be an open space of \(X\). Any open of \(Y\) is an open of \(X\), hence a functor \(\varphi : \mathrm{Ouv}(Y) \to \mathrm{Ouv}(X)\). The functor \(\varphi\) is continuous (1.6). The functor \(\varphi_s : \mathrm{Ouv}(X)^{\sim} \to \mathrm{Ouv}(Y)^{\sim}\) is the “restriction to \(Y\)” functor. The functor \(\varphi^s_{\mathrm{ab}} : \mathrm{Ouv}(Y)^{\sim} \to \mathrm{Ouv}(X)^{\sim}\) (II 6.3.3, I 1.7) is the “extension by zero outside \(Y\)” functor introduced in [TF]. The functor \(\varphi^s : \mathrm{Ouv}(Y)^{\sim} \to \mathrm{Ouv}(X)^{\sim}\) (1.3) is analogous to the functor “extend by zero”; for any beam \(H\) on \(Y\) and all point \(y \in Y\), the fiber at \(y\) of \(\varphi^s H\) is canonically isomorphic to the fiber in \(y\) from \(H\); for any point \(x \in X\), \(x \notin Y\), the fiber at \(x\) of \(\varphi^s H\) is empty. The functor \(\varphi^s\) is called the “vacuum extension” functor. outside of \(Y\)”. The functor \(\varphi^s\) commutes to fiber products and products finite on a non-empty set of indices, but it does not transform the final object of \(\mathrm{Ouv}(Y)^{\sim}\) to the final object of \(\mathrm{Ouv}(X)^{\sim}\).

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 1.9.3

Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two small sites, \(u : C \to C'\) a functor between the underlying categories which transforms any covering family of \(C\) in covering family of \(C'\). The functor \(u\) is not continuous in general (see however 1.6). Here is a counterexample. Let \(S\) be a set of infinite cardinal of the universe \(\mathcal{U}\), and \(C\) the full subcategory of the category sets, whose objects are finite, non-empty subsets, from \(S\). We provide \(C\) with the canonical topology. It will be noted that families coverings of \(C\) are the surjective families of applications and that the covering families are not empty. We will further notice that there is, up to isomorphism, that two constant sheaves on \(C\): the sheaf of value the empty set and the value bundle the set with one element. Let \(C = C'\) and let \(u : C \to C'\) be a constant functor. The functor \(u\) transforms the covering families of \(C\) into covering families of \(C'\). The functor \(u\) is not continuous. Indeed, as the pre-beams represent tables of \(C'\) are sheaves, for any object \(X\) of \(C'\), there exists a beam \(F\) on \(C'\) such that the cardinality of the set \(F(X)\) is \(\geq 2\), and consequently the pre-beam \(u^* F\) is not a beam.

footnotes.html: (∗) “Covering” instead of “bicovering” was not necessarily enough, cf. example 1.9.3 below.
(∗) In fact, it is enough for \(u\) to commute to the fiber products occurring in base changes for morphisms coming from covering families for \(T\).
(1) indexed by the sets belonging to a suitable universe.
(2) It can in fact be shown that this condition is always satisfied.

blocks.html: 2. Cocontinuous functors

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.1

Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites and \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor between the underlying categories. We say that \(u\) is cocontinuous if it has the following property:

(COC) For any object \(Y\) of \(C\) and for any screen covering \(R \hookrightarrow u(Y)\), the sieve of \(Y\) generated by the arrows \(Z \to Y\) such that \(u(Z) \to u(Y)\) factors by \(R\), covers \(Y\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.2

Let \(C\) be a small site, \(C'\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(u : C \to C'\) a functor between the underlying categories. Let us note \(\widehat{u}_* : \widehat{C} \to \widehat{C}'\) the right-adjoint functor to the functor \(F \mapsto F \circ u = \widehat{u}^* F\) (I 5.1). The functor \(u\) is cocontinuous if and only if for any sheaf \(G\) on \(C\), \(\widehat{u}_* G\) is a beam on \(C'\).

Proof. The condition is sufficient. Suppose that for any sheaf \(G\) on \(C\), the pre-beam \(\widehat{u}_* G\) is a beam. Let \(Y\) be an object of \(C\) and \(R \hookrightarrow u(Y)\) a covering sieve. We have an isomorphism \(\mathrm{Hom}(u(Y), \widehat{u}_* G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(R, \widehat{u}_* G)\), hence by addition (I 5.1) an iso- morphism \(\mathrm{Hom}(\widehat{u}^* \circ u(Y), G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(\widehat{u}^* R, G)\). We deduce (II 5.3) that the morphism \(\widehat{u}^* R \to \widehat{u}^* u(Y)\) is bicovering. But the functor \(\widehat{u}^*\) commutes to projective limits and consequently the morphism \(\widehat{u}^* R \to \widehat{u}^* u(Y)\) is a monomorphism. It is therefore a covering monomorphism. On the other hand we has a canonical morphism \(Y \xrightarrow{\ p\ } \widehat{u}^* u(Y)\) deduced from the addition morphism \(\mathrm{id}_C \to \widehat{u}^* u\), (I 5.4 3)). Let's then do the base change \(Y \xrightarrow{\ p\ } \widehat{u}^* u(Y)\). We obtain a sieve covering \(Y\): \[ \widehat{u}^* R \times_{\widehat{u}^* u(Y)} Y \longrightarrow Y , \] and when we look for the arrows \(Z \to Y\) which are factorized by this sieve, we obtain the sieve described by the property (COC).

The condition is necessary: Let \(S\) be an object of \(C'\) and \(R \hookrightarrow S\) a sieve covering \(S\). We must demonstrate that for any sheaf \(G\) on \(C\) we have an isomorphism \[ \mathrm{Hom}(S, \widehat{u}_* G) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{Hom}(R, \widehat{u}_* G) . \] Then using proposition II 5.3 and isomorphisms of addition, we see that it is enough to demonstrate that the monomorphism \(\widehat{u}^* R \to \widehat{u}^* S\) is covering. To do this, it is enough to demonstrate (II 5.1) that for any change of base \(Y \to \widehat{u}^* S\) the sieve \(\widehat{u}^* R \times_{\widehat{u}^* S} Y\) is covering, (\(Y\) object of \(C\)). But, according to the properties of the functors adjoints and I 5.4 3), the morphism \(Y \to \widehat{u}^* S\) is factorized by \(Y \xrightarrow{\ p\ } \widehat{u}^* u(Y) \to \widehat{u}^* S\), and consequently the sieve \(\widehat{u}^* R \times_{\widehat{u}^* S} Y \to Y\) is the transformed by the base change \(Y \xrightarrow{\ p\ } \widehat{u}^* u(Y)\) of the monomorphism \(\widehat{u}^* R \times_{\widehat{u}^* S} \widehat{u}^* u(Y) \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \widehat{u}^* u(Y)\). But the functor \(\widehat{u}^*\) commutes at the limits projectives, and consequently the monomorphism \(q\) is the transformed by \(\widehat{u}^*\) of the sieve \(R \times_S u(Y) \hookrightarrow u(Y)\), which is covering. The hypothesis (COC) then allows us to say that the sieve \(\widehat{u}^* R \times_{\widehat{u}^* S} Y \to Y\) is covering, cqfd.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.3

Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites and \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor cocontinuous. Let us denote \(u^* : \widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C}\) the functor \(u^* = a \widehat{u}^* i'\), where \(a\) denotes the “associated bundle” functor for \(C\), \(\widehat{u}^*\) the functor \(F \mapsto F \circ u\), \(i'\) the inclusion functor \(\widetilde{C}' \to \widehat{C}'\).

  1. 1)

    The functor \(u^*\) commutes at inductive limits and is exact.

  2. 2)

    We have a canonical isomorphism \(u^* a' \simeq a \widehat{u}^*\), where \(a'\) denotes the “associated bundle” functor for \(\widehat{C}'\).

  3. 3)

    The functor \(u^*\) admits a right adjoint \(u_* : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}'\). When \(C\) is small, the diagram \[ (2.3.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]^-{u_*} \ar@{^{(}->}[d]_-{i} & \widetilde{C}' \ar@{^{(}->}[d]^-{i'} \\ \widehat{C} \ar[r]_-{\widehat{u}_*} & \widehat{C}' } \] where the bottom functor is right-adjoined to the functor \(\widehat{u}^*\) (I 5.1), is commutative up to canonical isomorphism.

  4. 4)

    Let \(\mathcal{V} \supset \mathcal{U}\) be a universe. Let us note \(u_{*\mathcal{U}} : \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \to \widetilde{C}'_{\mathcal{U}}\) (resp. \(u_{*\mathcal{V}} : \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}} \to \widetilde{C}'_{\mathcal{V}}\)) the right adjoint functor relating to the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) (resp. \(\mathcal{V}\)) whose existence tence is asserted in 3). The diagram \[ (2.3.2) \qquad \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{U}} \ar[r]^-{u_{*\mathcal{U}}} \ar[d] & \widetilde{C}'_{\mathcal{U}} \ar[d] \\ \widetilde{C}_{\mathcal{V}} \ar[r]_-{u_{*\mathcal{V}}} & \widetilde{C}'_{\mathcal{V}} } \] is commutative up to isomorphism.

Proof. Assertions 1) and 2) follow immediately from II 3.4. The assertion 3), when \(C\) is small, results from 2.2. When \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, it will be demonstrated in 4.4. Assertion 4), when \(C\) is small, results from the analogous commutativity assertion when the topologies on \(C\) and \(C'\) are chaotic and of I 3.5. When the topologies on \(C\) and \(C'\) are chaotic, the commutativity of (2.3.2) is immediately seen on the explicit description of \(u_*\) (I 5.1).

We will not develop here the considerations relating to \(\gamma\) - beam category objects. It will suffice for us to note that the functors \(u^*\) and \(u_*\) introduced in this number always commute with finite projective limits (contrary to what happened in the previous number for the functor \(u^s\)). As a result, they continue naturally into functors defined on the \(\gamma\)-objects, which are adjoint one from the other and which commute to the “overall bundle” functors underlying”. We will do the notation abuses reported in 1.8, con- Please note by \(u^*\) and \(u_*\) the extensions to \(\gamma\)-objects.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.5

Let \(C\) and \(C'\) be two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites and \(C \overset{v}{\underset{u}{\rightleftarrows}} C'\) be a couple of functors, where \(v\) is adjoint to the left of \(u\). The following properties are equivalent:

  1. i)

    The functor \(u\) is continuous.

  2. ii)

    The functor \(v\) is cocontinuous.

Moreover, under these equivalent conditions, we have canonical isomorphisms \(v_* \simeq u_s\), \(v^* \simeq u^s\). In particular, the functor \(u^s\) commutes at the limits finite projectives.

Proof. The property for a functor to be continuous or cocontinuous does not does not depend on the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) for which \(C\) and \(C'\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites (this is immediately in the case of a cocontinuous functor, and this follows from 1.5 in the case of a continuous functor). We can therefore, even if it means increasing the universe, assume that \(C\) and \(C'\) are small. The proposition then results of 2.2 and I 5.6.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 2.6

Let \(u : C \to C'\) be a continuous and cocontinuous functor between two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites. Then the functor \(u^* : \widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C}\) (2.3) commutes to \(\mathcal{U}\)-limits inductive and projective. Let us note \(u_! : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}'\) and \(u_* : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}'\) the functors adjoint to \(u^*\) on the left and on the right respectively. The functor \(u_!\) is fully faithful if and only if the functor \(u_*\) is fully faithful. When \(u\) is fully faithful, \(u_!\) is fully faithful and the converse is true when the topologies of \(C\) and \(C'\) are less fine than the canonical topology.

The functor \(u^*\) admits a right adjoint \(u_*\) (2.3) and an adjoint left \(u_!\) (1.2). The functor \(u^*\) therefore commutes at inductive limits and projectives (I 2.11). The fact that the functor \(u_!\) is fully faithful if and only if the functor \(u_*\) is fully faithful is a general property of adjoint functors (I 5.7.1). When \(u\) is fully faithful, the functor \(\widehat{u}_* : \widehat{C}_{\mathcal{V}} \to \widehat{C}'_{\mathcal{V}}\) relating to the \(\mathcal{V}\)-presheaves, for a large enough universe \(\mathcal{V}\) is fully faithful (I 5.7). As a result the functor \(u_* : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}'\) is fully faithful by virtue of the commu- tativity of (2.3.1) and (2.3.2), so \(u_!\) is fully faithful according to the above. The converse is deduced from the existence of the diagram commutative 1.2 iv), taking into account the fact that the functors \(\varepsilon_C\) and \(\varepsilon_{C'}\) are fully faithful when the topologies are less fine than the topo- canonical logy, cqfd.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.html:
Example 2.7

With the notations of 1.9.2, the functor \[ \varphi : \mathrm{Ouv}(Y) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ouv}(X) \] is continuous (1.9.2) and cocontinuous (2.2). Additionally, be \(i : Y \to X\) the canonical injection and \(i^{-1} : \mathrm{Ouv}(X) \to \mathrm{Ouv}(Y)\) the functor that it allows you to define (1.9.1). The functor \(i^{-1}\) is right adjoint to the functor \(\varphi\). We therefore have a sequence of three adjoint functors: \[ \begin{array}{ccc} \varphi^s & , & \varphi_s \\ & & \| \\ & & \varphi^* \ , \ \varphi_* \end{array} \] and canonical isomorphisms \(\varphi^* \simeq (i^{-1})^s\), \(\varphi_* \simeq i^{-1}_s\).
3. Induced topology

Let \(C'\) be a site, \(C\) be a category and \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor. For any universe \(\mathcal{U}\) such that \(C'\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-small category- gorie, let us denote by \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) the finest among the topologies \(T\) on \(C\) which make \(u\) continuous (1.1). (Such a topology exists thanks to II 2.2). The topology \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) does not depend on the universe \(\mathcal{U}\). Indeed, if \(\mathcal{V} \supset \mathcal{U}\) is a universe we have \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}} = \mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{V}}\) (1.1 and 1.5). The topology \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) is called the topology induced on \(C\) by the topology of \(C'\) by means of the functor \(u\)(1).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.2

Let \(C\) be a small category, \(C'\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(u : C \to C'\) a functor, \(\mathscr{C}\) the topology on \(C\) induced by \(u\). Let \(X\) be an object of \(C\) and \(R \hookrightarrow X\) a screen of \(X\). The following properties are equivalent:

  1. i)

    The screen \(R \hookrightarrow X\) is covering for \(\mathscr{C}\).

  2. ii)

    For any change to base \(Y \to X\), where \(Y\) is an object of \(C\), the morphism \(u_!(R \times_X Y) \to u(Y)\) is bicovering in \(\widehat{C}'\) (II 5.2).

Proof. i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii) results from the axiom (T1) of topologies and from 1.2. ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i): For any beam \(F\) on \(C'\), the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(Y, u^* F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(R \times_X Y, u^* F) \] is isomorphic, by addition (I 5.1), to the application \[ \mathrm{Hom}(u(Y), F) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Hom}(u_!(R \times_X Y), F) \] which is bijective (II 5.3). As a result (II 2.2) the sieve \(R \hookrightarrow X\) is covering for \(\mathscr{C}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.3

Let \(C'\) be a site, \(C\) be a category, \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor, \(\mathscr{C}\) the topology on \(C\) induced by the topology of \(C'\). Or \((X_i \to X)\) \(i \in I\) a family of quarable morphisms of \(C\) and suppose that \(u\) commutes to the pro- fiber ducts.(∗) The following properties are equivalent:

  1. i)

    The family \(X_i \to X\), \(i \in I\), is covering for \(\mathscr{C}\).

  2. ii)

    The family \((u(X_i) \to u(X))\), \(i \in I\), is covering.

Proof. i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii) follows from 1.6. ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i): Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe such that \(C\) either \(\mathcal{U}\)-small and \(C'\) a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site. Let \(R \hookrightarrow X\) be the sieve generated by the \(X_i \to X\). The pre-bundle \(R\) is the core of the pair of arrows (I 2.12 and I 3.5) \[ \coprod_{i,j} X_i \times_X X_j \rightrightarrows \coprod_i X_i \] (the direct sum is taken here in \(\widehat{C}\)). As the functor \(u_!\) commutes at the inductive limits (I 5.4), the pre-bundle \(u_! R\) is the co-core of the pair of arrows \[ \coprod_{i,j} u(X_i \times_X X_j) \rightrightarrows \coprod_i u(X_i) . \] As the functor \(u\) commutes to fiber products, the presheaf \(u_! R\) is the core of the pair of arrows \[ \coprod_{i,j} u(X_i) \times_{u(X)} u(X_j) \rightrightarrows \coprod_i u(X_i) , \] and consequently \(u_! R \to u(X)\) is a sieve of \(u(X)\) generated by the \(u(X_i) \to u(X)\), \(i \in I\). Using once again the fact that \(u\) switches to fiber products, we show that for any change of base \(Y \to X\), where \(Y\) is an object of \(C\), \(u_!(R \times_X Y) \to u(Y)\) is a sieve of \(u(Y)\) generated by \(u(X_i) \times_{u(X)} u(Y) \to u(Y)\), \(i \in I\). Like the \(u(X_i) \to u(X)\) family, \(i \in I\), is covering, the family \(u(X_i) \times_{u(X)} u(Y) \to u(Y)\), \(i \in I\), is covering. So \(u_!(R \times_X Y) \to u(Y)\) is a covering sieve and therefore (3.2) \(R \hookrightarrow X\) is covering for \(\mathscr{C}\), cqfd.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 3.4

Let \(C'\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(C\) be a full subcategory of \(C'\), \(u : C \to C'\) the inclusion functor. It is assumed that fiber products are representable in \(C\) and that \(u\) commutes to fiber products. The following conditions are equivalent:

  1. i)

    a) For any object \(X\) of \(C\), any covering family \((Y_j \to X)\), \(j \in J\), of \(C'\) is increased by a covering family \(X_i \to X\), \(i \in I\), where the \(X_i\) are objects of \(C\).

    b) There exists a small set \(G\) of objects of \(C\), such that any object of \(C\) is the goal of a covering family in \(C'\) of morphisms whose sources are in \(G\).

  2. ii)

    The topology induced by the topology of \(C'\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology (II 3.0.2) and the functor \(u\) is continuous and cocontinuous for this topology.
Proof.Follows immediately from 3.3 and 2.1.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.5

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(\varepsilon_C : C \to \widetilde{C}\) be the canonical functor (II 4.4.0). Let's equip \(\widetilde{C}\) with the canonical topology. So the topology of the site \(C\) is the topology induced by the topology of \(\widetilde{C}\).

Proof. The covering families of \(\widetilde{C}\) for the canonical topology are the families universal effective epimorphic families (II 2.5), i.e. (II 4.3) the epimor-families phics. Let \(T\) be the topology of the site \(C\) and \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) be the finest topology of the topo- ologies \(T'\) on \(C\) such that for any beam \(F\) on \(\widetilde{C}\), \(F \circ \varepsilon_C\) is a beam for \(T'\). It suffices to show that \(T = \mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\), because then \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology and hence \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) is the induced topology.

A) The topology \(T\) is finer than \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\): Let \((X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), a family covering of \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\). Then for any beam \(F\) on \(\widetilde{C}\), \(F(\varepsilon_C(X)) \to \prod_i F(\varepsilon_C(X_i))\) is injective and consequently (II 5.2) \((\varepsilon_C X_i \to \varepsilon_C X)\), \(i \in I\), is covering for the canonical topology of \(\widetilde{C}\), therefore (II 5.2) \((X_i \to X)\), \(i \in I\), is covering for \(T\).

B) The topology \(T\) is less fine than \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\): It is enough to show that \(\varepsilon_C : C \to \widetilde{C}\) is continuous (1.1). Now we will demonstrate in IV n° 1 that any beam on \(\widetilde{C}\) is represented sensible and consequently, for any beam \(F\) on \(\widetilde{C}\), \(F \circ \varepsilon_C\) is a beam on \(C\). (We will not use 3.5 until IV 1).
Let us note two results which will be used in VI 7.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.6

Let \((C_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of sites, \(C\) be a category and for any \(i \in I\), \(u_i : C_i \to C\) a functor, \(\mathcal{U}\) a universe such as the categories \(C_i\) and \(C\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. There exists a topology \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) on \(C\) which is the thinnest of the to- pologies for which the \(u_i\) are continuous. The topology \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) does not depend on the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) for which the categories considered are small.
The last assertion of 3.6 follows from 1.5.

Let \(T\) be a topology on \(C\). Then the functors \(u_i\) are continuous if and only if for all \(i \in I\) and for all sieve covering \(R \hookrightarrow X\) of an object \(X\) of \(C_i\), the morphism \[ u_{i!}(R) \longrightarrow u_i(X) \] of \(\widehat{C}\) is bi-covering (1.2 (ii)). So 3.6 is a consequence of

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.6.1

Let \(C\) be a small category, \((u_i : F_i \to G_i)_{i \in I}\) a family of arrows of \(\widehat{C}\). Then there exists on \(C\) a topology that is the least fine among those which make the morphisms \(u_i\) covering (resp. bicovering) (II 5.2).

Saying that \(u : F \to G\) is covering for a given topology \(T\) means that for any arrow \(X \to G\), with \(X \in \mathrm{Ob}\, C\), the corresponding arrow \(F \times_G X \to X\) is covering, or even that the family of arrows \(X' \to X\) of \(C\) which is factori- felt by the previous arrow is covering. The fact that there exists a topology least fine among those for which the \(u_i : F_i \to G_i\) are covering results therefore from I 1.1.6, hence the assertion not respected in 3.6.1. The relevant assertion follows from this duit, remembering that a morphism \(u : F \to G\) is bicovering if and only if the morphisms \(u : F \to G\) and \(\mathrm{diag}_u : F \to F \times_G F\) are covering.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 3.7

Let \((C_i)_{i \in I}\) be a family of sites, \(C\) be a category, for all \(i \in I\), \(u_i : C_i \to C\) a functor, and \(\mathcal{U}\) a universe such that the categories \(C_i\) and \(C\) are \(\mathcal{U}\)-small. There exists a topology \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) which is the finest for which the \(u_i\) are cocontinuous. The topology \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) does not depend on the universe \(\mathcal{U}\) for which the categories considered are small.

Let \(\mathcal{U}\) be a universe for which the categories considered are small. The functors \(u_i\) are cocontinuous for a topology \(\mathscr{C}\) of \(C\) if and only if for any \(i \in I\) and any beam \(F\) on \(C_i\) the pre-beam \(\widehat{u}_{i*} F\) is a beam for \(\mathscr{C}\) (2.2). It follows that the topology \(\mathscr{C}_{\mathcal{U}}\) is the finest topology for which the pre-sheaves \(\widehat{u}_{i*} F\), \(i \in I\), \(F \in \mathrm{ob}\, C_i\), are sheaves (II 2.2). The last assertion follows from 2.2.

footnotes.html: (1) When no confusion results this topology is called the topology induced on \(C\) by the topology of \(C'\).
(∗) In fact, it is enough that \(u\) commutes to fiber products of the form \(X_i \times_X X'\).

blocks.html: 4. Comparison Lemma

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 4.1 (comparison lemma)

Let \(C\) be a small category, \(C'\) a site whose underlying category is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-category and \(u : C \to C'\) a fully faithful functor. Let's equip \(C\) with the topology induced by \(u\) (3.1). Let's consider the properties:

  1. i)

    Any object from \(C'\) can be covered by objects from \(C\).

  2. ii)

    The functor \(F \mapsto F \circ u\) induces an equivalence of categories of the category of beams on \(C'\) in the category of beams on \(C\).

We always have i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii). When \(C'\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and when the topology of \(C'\) is less fine than the canonical topology, we have ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i).
Let's first prove i) \(\Longrightarrow\) ii). The demonstration is done in two steps.

First step. For any presheaf \(H\) of \(\widehat{C}'\) the addition morphism \(\varphi : u_! u^* H \to H\) (I 5.1) is bicovering (II 5.3) and the functor \(u_s : \widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C}\) (1.1.1) is fully faithful.

Let \(C/H\) be the small category whose objects are the objects \(Y\) of \(C\) provided with a morphism \(u(Y) \to H\), and whose morphisms are the commutative diagrams: \[ \xymatrix{ u(Y) \ar[rr]^-{u(m)} \ar[dr] & & u(Y') \ar[dl] \\ & H & } \] We have \(u^* H = \varinjlim_{Y \in \mathrm{ob}\, C/H} Y\) (I 3.4) and consequently (I 5.4) \(u_! u^* H = \varinjlim_{Y \in \mathrm{ob}\, C/H} u(Y)\). The addition morphism is the morphism obvious which results from the description of \(u_! u^* H\) as an inductive limit. The morphism \(\varphi\) has the following property: (∗) For any object \(Y\) of \(C\), any morphism \(m : u(Y) \to H\) uniquely factors into the canonical morphism \(\alpha(m) : u(Y) \to u_! u^* H\) and the morphism \(\varphi\). It follows immediately from i) that the morphism \(\varphi\) is covering (II 5.1), let us show that it is bicovering. Let \(p, q : Z \rightrightarrows u_! u^* H\) two morphisms of an object \(Z\) of \(\widehat{C}'\), such that \(\varphi p = \varphi q\). For any object \(Y\) of \(C\) and any morphism \(n : u(Y) \to Z\), we a \(\varphi p n = \varphi q n\). The property (∗) then results in \(p n = q n\), and as the \(u(Y)\) covers \(Z\), the core of \((p, q)\) is a sieve covering \(Z\). The mor- phisme \(\varphi\) is therefore bicovering (II 5.3). For any beam \(H\) on \(C'\), \(u^* H\) is a beam on \(C\) denoted \(u_s H\) (1.1.1). We also have \(u^s u_s H = a' u_! u_s H\) (1.3), and the addition morphism \(u^s u_s H \to H\) is obtained by applying the functor “associated bundle” with the morphism \(\varphi : u_! u^* H \to H\). As a result (II 5.3) the addition morphism \(u^s u_s H \to H\) is an isomorphism. Therefore \(u_s : \widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C}\) is fully faithful.

Second step. The functor \(u\) is cocontinuous and the functor \(u^s : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}'\) (1.2) is fully faithful. Therefore \(u_s : \widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C}\) is an equivalence. Let \(Y\) be an object of \(C\) and \(i : R \hookrightarrow u(Y)\) be a covering sieve. Like the functor \(u^*\) commutes to projective limits (I 5.5) the morphism \(u^*(i) : u^*(R) \hookrightarrow u^* u(Y)\) is a monomorphism. As \(u\) is fully faithful, we have \(u^* u(Y) \simeq Y\), hence a sieve of \(Y\), \(u^*(i) : u^*(R) \to Y\). To show that \(u\) is cocontinuous, it suffices to show that \(u^*(i) : u^*(R) \to Y\) is a covering screen for the topology induced on \(C\) (2.1). For this, it suffices to show (3.2) that for any change of base \(m : X \to Y\), the morphism \(u_!(u^*(R) \times_Y X) \to u(X)\) is bicovering. But as \(u^*\) commutes at projective limits, we have \(u^*(R) \times_Y X = u^*(R \times_{u(Y)} u(X))\) and \(R \times_{u(Y)} u(X)\) is a sieve covering \(u(X)\). It is therefore sufficient to show that for any object \(Y\) of \(C\) and any sieve covering \(i : R \hookrightarrow u(Y)\), the morphism of \(\widehat{C}'\), \(u_! u^*(R) \xrightarrow{\ u_! u^*(i)\ } u(Y)\), is dual-covering. Now this morphism is factorized into the addition morphism \(u_! u^*(R) \to R\), which is bicovering (first not), and the monomorphism \(R \hookrightarrow u(Y)\) which is covering. It is therefore double covering (II 5.3). This shows that \(u\) is cocontinuous. As \(u\) is continuous and fully faithful, it results from 2.6 (used in the case where \(C\) is small) that \(u^s\) (noted \(u_!\) in 2.6) is fully faithful. As \(u^s\) and \(u_s\) are assistants of each other and that they are fully faithful, they are quasi-inverse functors and hence \(u_s\) is an equivalence.

Let us now prove that ii) \(\Longrightarrow\) i). The objects \(Y\) of \(C\) form a generating family of \(\widetilde{C}\) (II 4.10) and consequently, for any object \(X\) of \(C'\), there is an epimorphic family (in \(\widetilde{C}\)) \(v_i : Y_i \to u_s X\), \(i \in I\). We have \(u_s u(Y_i) = Y_i\) and, the functor \(u_s\) being an equivalence of categories, we have \(v_i = u_s(w_i)\), where the \(w_i : u(Y_i) \to X\) form a epimorphic family of \(\widetilde{C}'\). It then follows from II 5.1 that the family \(w_i : u(Y_i) \to X\), \(i \in I\), is covering.
4.2. End of 1.2 demo.

Let \(G\) be a full subcategory of \(C\) whose objects form a small topologically generating family of \(C\) (II 3.0.1). Munitions \(G\) of the topology induced by the inclusion functor \(i : G \to C\) (3.1). The functor \((u \circ i)_s = i_s \circ u_s\) (1.1.1) admits a left adjoint according to the first part of the demonstration of 1.2. As \(i_s\) is an equivalence of categories (4.1), the functor \(u_s\) admits a left adjoint \(u^s\) and we has a functorial isomorphism \(u^s \simeq (u \circ i)^s \circ i_s\). Let us show that the diagram \[ (4.2.1) \qquad \xymatrix{ C \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{\varepsilon_C} & C' \ar[d]^-{\varepsilon_{C'}} \\ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{u^s} & \widetilde{C}' } \] is commutative up to isomorphism. For any beam \(H\) on \(C'\), we have \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\widetilde{C}'}(\varepsilon_{C'} u X, H) \simeq u_s H(X)\), for all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\). We also have \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\widetilde{C}'}(u^s \varepsilon_C X, H) \simeq \mathrm{Hom}_{\widetilde{C}}(\varepsilon_C X, u_s H)\) by addition, then \(\mathrm{Hom}_{\widetilde{C}}(\varepsilon_C X, u_s H) \simeq u_s H(X)\) by definition of \(\varepsilon_C\). Hence an isomorphism \(\varepsilon_{C'} u X \simeq u^s \varepsilon_C X\) for all \(X \in \mathrm{ob}\, C\). This demonstrates i) \(\Longrightarrow\) iv). Let's show that iv) \(\Longrightarrow\) i). We have a commutative diagram \[ (4.2.2) \qquad \xymatrix{ G \ar[r]^-{i} \ar[d]_-{\varepsilon_G} & C \ar[r]^-{u} \ar[d]_-{\varepsilon_C} & C' \ar[d]^-{\varepsilon_{C'}} \\ \widetilde{G} \ar[r]_-{i^s} & \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{u^s} & \widetilde{C}' , } \] and consequently, according to the first part of the demonstration, the functor \(i \circ u : G \to C'\) is continuous. We immediately deduce from 4.1 and 1.1 that \(u\) is continuous, hence iv) \(\Longrightarrow\) i). We further obtain the uniqueness of \(u^s\); knowing, by the first part of the demonstration, the uniqueness when \(C\) is small.
4.3. End of demonstration of 1.5 and 1.7.

Let \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor between two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites and \(G\) be a full subcategory of \(C\) whose set of objects is a small topologically generating family of \(C\) (II 3.0.1). Let's equip \(G\) with the topology induced by the inclusion functor \(i : G \to C\). It results immediately from 4.1 and 1.1 that \(u\) is continuous if and only if \(u \circ i\) is continuous. From this remark and the first part of the demonstration of 1.5 results in the general case. This remark allows also to reduce the demonstration to 1.7 in case the category \(C\) is small.
4.4. End of 2.3 demo.

Let \(u : C \to C'\) be a functor between two \(\mathcal{U}\)-sites, \(G\) be a sub- category full of \(C\) whose set of objects is a small family topologically generating \(C\) (II 3.0.1), provided with the induced topology by the inclusion functor \(i : G \to C\). It results from the demonstration from 4.1 (4.1.1 second step) that \(i\) is cocontinuous. As a result, when \(u\) is cocontinuous, the functor \(u \circ i : G \to C'\) is cocontinuous. Moreover, \(i^* : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{G}\) is an equivalence of categories (4.1). So the functor \(u^* : \widetilde{C}' \to \widetilde{C}\) admits an assistant on the right. This demonstrates 3). To demonstrate 4), it suffices to note that \((u \circ i)_* \simeq u_* \circ i_*\) and that \(i_*\) is an equi- valence (4.1). The commutativity of (2.3.2) then results from the commutativity avoidance of these diagrams when \(C\) is small.
5. Location

Now let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site and \(X\) be an object of \(\widehat{C}\). Unless otherwise noted expressly to the contrary the category \(C/X\) will be provided with the topology \(\mathscr{C}\) induced by the functor \(j_X : C/X \to C\) (3.1). The notation \(C/X\) will designate the category \(C/X\) with the topology \(\mathscr{C}\). Proposition I 5.11 we shows that the functor \(j_{X!}\) commutes to fiber products and consequently trans- forms any monomorphism into a monomorphism. In particular, for all object \((Y \to X)\) of \(C/X\), the functor \(j_{X!}\) establishes a one-to-one correspondence invokes between the screens, in the category \(C/X\), of the object \((Y \to X)\) and the sieves, in \(C\), of the object \(Y\).

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.2

Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(X\) be an object of \(\widehat{C}\) and \(j_X : C/X \to C\) the corresponding continuous functor.

  1. 1)

    A screen \(R\) of an object \((Z \to X)\) is covering in \(C/X\) if and only if the screen \(j_{X!}(R) \hookrightarrow Z\) is covering in \(C\).

  2. 2)

    The functor \(j_X : C/X \to C\) is cocontinuous and continuous.

  3. 3)

    Let \(m : Y \to X\) be a morphism of \(\widehat{C}\). We then have the diagram commutative: \[ \xymatrix{ C/Y \ar[rr]^-{j_m} \ar[dr]_-{j_Y} & & C/X \ar[dl]^-{j_X} \\ & C & } \] The topology induced by the functor \(j_Y\) on \(C/Y\) is equal to the topology induced by \(j_m\) on \(C/Y\).

  4. 4)

    The topology induced by \(j_X : C/X \to C\) is a \(\mathcal{U}\)-topology (II 3.0.2).
Proof. 1) If the sieve \(R\) of \((Z \to X)\) is covering, the sieve \(j_{X!}(R) \hookrightarrow Z\) is covering (1.6).
Conversely, if the screen \(j_{X!}(R) \to Z\) is covering in \(C\), we see that it is the same for any sieve obtained by making a base change in \(C/X\). The screen \(R\) is therefore covering (3.2).
2) Deduced immediately from 1) by applying 2.1.
3) Can be immediately deduced from the description of the screens coverings given by 1).

4) Let \((G)_i\), \(i \in I\), a small topologically general family trice of \(C\). We immediately check that the little family \((u : G_i \to X)\), \(u \in \coprod_{i \in I} \mathrm{Hom}_{\widehat{C}}(G_i, X)\), is topologically generating in \(C/X\).
Terminology and notations 5.3.

According to the previous proposition the functor \(j_X\) is both a continuous and cocontinuous functor. It therefore defines a sequence of three adjoint functors (4.3.2) between the categories of sheaves of sets (1.3 and 2.3): \[ \begin{aligned} j_X^s \ &: \ (C/X)^{\sim} \to \widetilde{C} \\ j_X^* = j_{X,s} \ &: \ \widetilde{C} \to (C/X)^{\sim} \\ j_{X*} \ &: \ (C/X)^{\sim} \to \widetilde{C} . \end{aligned} \] In the particular situation of proposition 5.2 we We will use the following terminology and notations:

1. 1)

    The functor \(j_{X*}\) will be called the direct image functor.

  2. 2)

    The functor \(j_{X,s} = j_X^*\) will be denoted \(j_X^*\) and will be called the restricted functor tion to \(C/X\).

  3. 3)

    The functor \(j_X^s\) on the set sheaves will be called the “ functor extension by the void to the category \(C\) » and will be denoted \(j_{X!}\) (cf. 1.9.2).
We therefore have a sequence of three adjoint functors between \((C/X)^{\sim}\) and \(\widetilde{C}\): \[ j_{X!} \ , \ j_X^* \ , \ j_{X*} . \]

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.4

The functor \(j_{X!} : (C/X)^{\sim} \to \widetilde{C}\) is factorized by the category \(\widetilde{C}/aX\) (\(a\) is the associated beam functor): \[ (C/X)^{\sim} \xrightarrow{\ e_X^{\sim}\ } \widetilde{C}/aX \to \widetilde{C} . \] The functor: \[ e_X^{\sim} \ : \ (C/X)^{\sim} \longrightarrow \widetilde{C}/aX \] is an equivalence of categories. The functor restriction to \(C/X\), com- posed with the equivalence \(e_X^{\sim}\), is isomorphic to the functor “change of base by \(aX \to e\)” (\(e\) the final object of \(\widetilde{C}\)): \(F \mapsto (F \times aX \xrightarrow{\ \mathrm{pr}_2\ } aX)\).

Proof. The image by \(j_{X!}\) of the final object of \((C/X)^{\sim}\) is the object \(aX\); hence factorization. To show that the functor \(e_X^{\sim}\) is an equivalence, we will limit ourselves to a few indications. According to I 5.11, a pre- beam on \(C/X\) is defined by a pre-beam \(F\) on \(C\) provided with a morphism \(F \to X\). We then demonstrate that the following two properties are equi- valents:

1. i)

    The pre-beam on \(C/X\) defined by \(F \to X\) is a beam.

  2. ii)

    The following diagram is Cartesian (we denote by \(i\) and \(a\) the functors injection into the pre-bundles, and associated beam): \[ \xymatrix{ F \ar[r] \ar[d] & i a F \ar[d] \\ X \ar[r] & i a X , } \]
We then immediately deduce that \(e_X^{\sim}\) is an equivalence. The last assertion is trivial.

blocks.title: Proposal
blocks.html:
Proposal 5.5

1) Let \(C\) be a \(\mathcal{U}\)-site, \(X\) be an object of \(\widehat{C}\). The diagram below of categories and functors is commutative to isomorphisms canonicals near: \[ \xymatrix@C=2.6em{ C/X \ar[r]^-{h_X} \ar@{=}[d] & (C/X)^{\wedge} \ar[r]^-{a_X} \ar[d]^-{e_X^{\wedge}} & (C/X)^{\sim} \ar[r]^-{i_X} \ar[d]^-{e_X^{\sim}} & (C/X)^{\wedge} \ar[d]^-{e^{\wedge}/X} & \\ C/X \ar[r]_-{h/X} & \widehat{C}/X \ar[r]_-{a/X} & \widetilde{C}/aX \ar[r]_-{i/aX} & \widehat{C}/iaX \ar[r]_-{\pi} & \widehat{C}/X . } \] The notations \(a_X\) and \(i_X\) denote the “sheaf” functors associated” and injection into the pre-bundles for the site \(C/X\). The note- tion \(a/X\) designates the natural extension of the functor \(a\) (associated bundle ciated for the site \(C\)) to the category of goal arrows \(X\), the same for the notation \(i/aX\). Finally the notation \(\pi\) designates the change functor base by the canonical arrow \(X \to iaX\).

2) Let \(m : Y \to X\) be a morphism of \(\widehat{C}\). The diagram below is commutative up to canonical isomorphism: \[ \xymatrix@C=3em{ \widetilde{C}/aX/aY \ar@{=}[dd] & & (C/X)^{\sim}/a_X Y \ar[ll]_-{f} \ar[dr]^-{g} & \\ & & & (C/X/Y)^{\sim} \ar[dl]^-{e_m^{\sim}} \\ \widetilde{C}/aY & & (C/Y)^{\sim} \ar[ll]^-{e_Y^{\sim}} & } \]

The arrow \(f\) is the natural extension of the equivalence \(e^{\sim}/X\) to the category of goal arrows \(a_X Y\), and is therefore an equi- valence of categories. The arrow \(g\) is none other than the equivalence of 5.4 applied to the situation \((C/X)/[m]\).

3) Let us denote by \(j_{aX!} : \widetilde{C}/aX \to \widetilde{C}\) the functor "let's forget \(aX\)", and by \(j_{aX}^* : \widetilde{C} \to \widetilde{C}/aX\) the functor "produced by \(aX\)". The following diagrams are commutative up to canonical isomorphism: \[ \xymatrix{ (C/X)^{\sim} \ar[r]^-{j_{X!}} \ar[d] & \widetilde{C} \ar@{=}[d] \\ \widetilde{C}/aX \ar[r]_-{j_{aX!}} & \widetilde{C} } \qquad \xymatrix{ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]^-{j_X^*} \ar@{=}[d] & (C/X)^{\sim} \ar[d]^-{e^{\sim}/X} \\ \widetilde{C} \ar[r]_-{j_{aX}^*} & \widetilde{C}/aX } \]
Proof. The proof of these assertions is immediate from the definition equivalences \(e_X^{\sim}\) (5.4) and \(e_X^{\wedge}\) (I 5.11).
